prs.title: [WIP] Add TRITON_MLA_SPARSE backend for SM80 sparse MLA support
prs.author: haosdent
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Closes #38006. Enables sparse MLA models (GLM-5, DeepSeek-V3.2) on SM80 (A100/A800) and SM121 (GB10/DGX Spark), where DeepGEMM / FlashMLA-Sparse / FlashInfer-MLA-Sparse are unavailable.

Three changes:

1. **Guard fix.** `has_deep_gemm()` (package check) → `is_deep_gemm_supported()` (SM90+ arch check) in `sparse_attn_indexer.py` and `indexer.py`. Prevents DeepGEMM SM90+ kernels from being invoked on SM80/SM121 when the Python package happens to be installed.

2. **Triton indexer MQA logits.** `mqa_logits_triton.py` reproduces DeepGEMM's `fp8_mqa_logits` / `fp8_paged_mqa_logits` (bf16 tensor-core matmul with fp32 accumulator + scale, ReLU + per-head weighting, sum across heads). PyTorch reference stays in `mqa_logits_fallback.py`. On GB10, `fp8_paged_mqa_logits` at ctx=2048 goes from 6.4 ms (torch loop) → 0.03 ms (~250×), saving ~380 ms/token across 60 sparse layers.

3. **New `TRITON_MLA_SPARSE` backend** (`triton_mla_sparse.py` → `triton_sparse_mla_kernel.py`). Single pure-Triton kernel with split-KV for low-batch decode (`num_kv_splits > 1` when the single-pass grid under-fills the device) and a single-pass fast path otherwise. Stage 2 merges per-split `(out, lse)` tiles via online-softmax rescaling (pattern from `triton_decode_attention.py`). `@triton.autotune` over `(num_warps, num_stages)` × `(index_topk, NUM_KV_SPLITS)`; warmed in `TritonMLASparseImpl.__init__` so the first user request does not pay the sweep cost. Registered in `AttentionBackendEnum` and added to the non-SM100 MLA priority list.

**Kernel benchmark on NVIDIA A30 (SM80, 56 SMs):**

| shape | XPU kernel | this PR | speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| nt=1, topk=2048, H=128 (decode) | 0.297 ms | 0.094 ms | **3.15×** |
| nt=1, topk=4096, H=128 | 0.580 ms | 0.167 ms | 3.46× |
| nt=1, topk=4096, H=16 | 0.582 ms | 0.081 ms | 7.17× |
| nt=128, topk=2048, H=128 (prefill) | 4.912 ms | 4.872 ms | 1.01× |

Decode (nt=1) across all (topk, H): median **4.30×**, best 7.17×. Non-decode (nt ≥ 8): autotu

prs.title: [Feature] Support custom callable proposer backend for speculative decoding
prs.author: CynicDora
prs.body_head:
**Motivation**
Currently, the speculative decoding framework in vLLM primarily relies on loading a complete, separate HuggingFace model as the drafter. However, researchers and systems engineers often need to experiment with custom, lightweight drafting strategies (such as n-gram matching, retrieval-based drafting, or MCTS heuristics) without the overhead of loading an additional full model.

Based on reviewer feedback, this PR integrates a `custom_class` proposer backend into the core engine. Rather than using an arbitrary callable function, it allows users to pass a module path to a custom Proposer class (e.g., `--custom-proposer-backend my_module.MyCustomProposer`). The engine dynamically imports and instantiates this class, natively integrating with its `.propose()` method. This ensures full interface compliance with vLLM's internal proposers while providing a robust, programmable hook for custom speculative decoding logic.

**Modifications**
* Enabled the initialization and routing of `--custom-proposer-backend` within the speculative decoding configuration.
* Implemented `CustomClassProposer` to handle dynamic imports, instantiation, and strict `.propose()` interface enforcement.
* Ensured proper tensor shaping (`[batch_size, draft_len]`) and device synchronization for the outputs generated by the custom class.
* Added a rigorous integration test (`tests/spec_decode/test_custom_proposer.py`) featuring a `DummyDraftProposer` class to validate the end-to-end tensor pipeline and acceptance logic.

**Verification / Test Results**
I have thoroughly verified this integration locally on a single GPU using `facebook/opt-125m`. The engine successfully compiles (with eager mode enforced), hooks into the dummy draft class during the generation loop, and outputs accurate text without any shape mismatches or memory leaks.

<summary>Click to expand the local integration test logs</summary>

prs.title: [Feat] dflash support for ROCm
prs.author: hangy-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

There's already dflash support for nvidia https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/36847. This PR is for dflash support for ROCm

## Benchmark

Model: `Qwen/Qwen3-8B`
Drafter: `z-lab/Qwen3-8B-DFlash-b16`
Num prompts per run: `8`
Max new tokens per request: `128`
Concurrencies: `[32, 16, 8, 1]`
Datasets (official dflash benchmark): `['gsm8k', 'math500', 'humaneval', 'mbpp', 'mt-bench']`
GPU: `AMD MI300`

I measured the following speedups using the DFlash methodology (https://github.com/z-lab/dflash)

### gsm8k

| conc | baseline tok/s | dflash tok/s | speedup |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 32 | 382.88 | 938.71 | 2.452x |
| 16 | 275.44 | 1064.00 | 3.863x |
| 8 | 395.10 | 1030.10 | 2.607x |
| 1 | 69.73 | 145.81 | 2.091x |

### math500

| conc | baseline tok/s | dflash tok/s | speedup |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 32 | 401.03 | 1079.76 | 2.692x |
| 16 | 366.08 | 997.11 | 2.724x |
| 8 | 394.34 | 1460.42 | 3.703x |
| 1 | 76.43 | 224.46 | 2.937x |

### humaneval

| conc | baseline tok/s | dflash tok/s | speedup |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 32 | 405.17 | 922.34 | 2.276x |
| 16 | 385.34 | 868.47 | 2.254x |
| 8 | 398.33 | 1253.89 | 3.148x |
| 1 | 76.06 | 200.50 | 2.636x |

### mbpp

| conc | baseline tok/s | dflash tok/s | speedup |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 32 | 398.50 | 686.99 | 1.724x |
| 16 | 405.31 | 537.49 | 1.326x |
| 8 | 407.93 | 933.81 | 2.289x |
| 1 | 77.89 | 206.02 | 2.645x |

### mt-bench

| conc | baseline tok/s | dflash tok/s | speedup |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 32 | 383.87 | 1172.04 | 3.053x |
| 16 | 375.00 | 514.22 | 1.371x |
| 8 | 387.94 | 423.75 | 1.092x |
| 1 | 75.99 | 82.47 | 1.085x |

## Known issue

For now, the AITER API `flash_attn_with_kvcache` couldn't support cuda graph. The issue has been reported to AITER team. They already have this fix PR:  https://github.com/ROCm/aiter/pull/2764. The benchmark data above is in `enforce-eager` mode.

prs.title: [ROCm] Add AITER RMSNorm+FP8 quantization fusion for MLA
prs.author: khairulkabir1661
prs.body_head:
## Summary

This PR adds support for AITER's fused RMSNorm + FP8 quantization kernel for DeepSeek MLA models on AMD ROCm, reducing quantization overhead.

### Key Features
- **Fused RMSNorm + FP8 Quantization**: Uses AITER's `fused_rms_fp8_group_quant` to combine RMSNorm and FP8 quantization in a single kernel
- **Pre-quantized Input Support**: Adds `input_scale`/`x_scale` parameter to linear layers to accept pre-quantized FP8 tensors
- **Reduced Quantization Steps**: Eliminates redundant quantization (3 steps → 2 steps)

## Changes

### Core Implementation
- **mla.py**: Added AITER fusion for RMSNorm + FP8 quantization when both AITER and FP8 quantization are enabled
- **linear.py**: Added `x_scale` parameter to `ReplicatedLinear`, `ColumnParallelLinear`, and `RowParallelLinear`
- **fp8.py**: Modified `Fp8LinearMethod.apply()` to accept and pass through `input_scale` parameter
- **fp8_utils.py**: Added support for pre-quantized FP8 inputs in AITER/Triton/Cutlass backends

### Testing
- Removed old test file
- Verified with GSM8K benchmark on DeepSeek-V3

## Performance Impact
- Reduces quantization overhead by fusing operations
- Avoids redundant quantization when input is already quantized
- ROCm-specific optimization for AMD GPUs

## Compatibility
- Only enabled when AITER is available AND FP8 quantization is configured
- Falls back to unfused path when AITER is not available
- No impact on other platforms or configurations

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

prs.title: [ROCm][Feature] Enable AITER MLA attention backend to work with Eagle3 speculative decoding on ROCm
prs.author: larryli2-amd
prs.body_head:
## Summary                                                                                      
                                                                                                  
  Enable AITER MLA attention backend to work with Eagle3 speculative decoding on ROCm.            
                                                                                                  
  AITER MLA is the fastest MLA attention backend on AMD MI300X/MI355X GPUs (using AMD's ASM       
  kernels via the aiter library). However, it currently cannot be used with speculative decoding
  methods like Eagle3 due to a `kernel_block_size` conflict:                                      
                                                                                                
  - **AITER MLA** declares `get_supported_kernel_block_sizes() = [1]`                             
  - **Eagle3 draft model** (flash_attn) requires `block_size = MultipleOf(16)`                    
  - Both share the same KV cache group, so `select_common_block_size()` fails — no block size      satisfies both constraints                                                                      
                                                 
  This means users must choose between AITER MLA (fast decode, no spec-dec) or a slower backend   
  (spec-dec works but lower baseline performance). This PR removes that limitation.               
                                                                                                  
  ## Key Insight                                                                                  
                                                 
  The aiter MLA decode kernel always operates with `page_size=1` internally — the vLLM wrapper    
  flattens `kv_buffer` via `.view(-1, 1, 1, head_size)` before calling the ASM kernel. The kernel
  never sees multi-token pages. So AITER MLA can work with any `kernel_block_size` — we just need 
  to exp

prs.title: [ROCm] Fix cu_seqlens_q off-by-one in AITER FA speculative decode path
prs.author: Bortlesboat
prs.body_head:
In the speculative decode path of `AiterFlashAttentionImpl` (when `decode_max_query_len > 1`), `cu_seqlens_q` is sliced as `query_start_loc[:num_decodes]`, giving `num_decodes` elements. But `cu_seqlens_q` for `unified_attention` is a cumulative length array that needs `num_seqs + 1` entries (including the leading 0).

The correct pattern is already used in the fallback path 33 lines later (line 1228-1229), which correctly does `[:num_decodes + 1]`.

Also fixed `descale_shape` which computed `shape[0] - 1` on the wrong-length slice, producing `num_decodes - 1` instead of `num_decodes`. Simplified to use `num_decodes` directly, matching line 1232.

No duplicate PRs found.

Testing:
- `uvx pre-commit run ruff-format --files vllm/v1/attention/backends/rocm_aiter_fa.py` (passes locally)
- Previous CI failure only requested the matching `ruff format` whitespace change on this line; this follow-up commit applies that exact diff.

AI assistance was used for code search and CI log review; all changes reviewed by the human submitter.

Signed-off-by: Bortlesboat <bortstheboat@gmail.com>

**Reference:** The correct `cu_seqlens_q` slicing is confirmed by the upstream AITER `unified_attention` implementation:
https://github.com/ROCm/aiter/blob/bc5ea32c19c604cda2f4781a97cb04d5fc494543/aiter/ops/triton/_triton_kernels/attention/unified_attention.py#L426
